title: Tomato Harvest Pricing Run Seed 42-1
scenario: Tomato Ketchup in Ontario. Supplier-to-manufacturer pricing sets the manufacturer cost basis before the retailer negotiation.

product_context.demand_signal: Retail ketchup demand is steady, but wholesale buyers want price protection before peak tomato procurement.
product_context.supply_signal: Tomato harvest yields are uneven across growing regions, putting pressure on paste input pricing.

agents.name: Supplier
agents.objective: Sell above the supplier reservation price while keeping volume committed.

agents.name: Manufacturer
agents.objective: Buy below the procurement ceiling and preserve downstream margin.

agents.name: Retailer
agents.objective: Buy enough units without exceeding the retail reservation price.

phases.name: supplier_manufacturer
phases.label: Supplier to Manufacturer
phases.description: The supplier negotiates an upstream unit price with the manufacturer.

phases.name: manufacturer_retailer
phases.label: Manufacturer to Retailer
phases.description: The manufacturer negotiates a downstream unit price with the retailer after the upstream deal sets its cost basis.

steps.message: Thanks for opening the discussion. Based on current market conditions, I can start at 6.38 per unit, which is in line with the latest market reference and gives us room to support committed volume reliably. If you’re looking at a meaningful volume commitment, I’d be open to discussing structure and potential flexibility from there. There is no buyer offer yet, so the reasonable move is to anchor the negotiation with an initial ask. The latest market reference is 6.11, so opening modestly above that preserves margin while leaving room to negotiate and keeping the conversation active.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn.

tool_calls.result_summary: Reviewed state for supplier_manufacturer round 1. Pending message: none.
Observed market reference price: 6.11.

steps.message: Thanks for the opening. That level is well above where we can realistically place volume on our side, so to get the conversation moving I can start at 2.06 per unit on an initial commitment. If there’s a path on volume, scheduling, or contract structure, we can keep working from there, but we’d need a very substantial adjustment from your opening to find overlap. The supplier opened far above our workable buy range, so accepting is not reasonable. Rather than end the discussion, a low counteroffer establishes our economic constraints early and leaves room to negotiate if they have flexibility.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn. Reservation-price violation: Manufacturer offered 2.06, below its hidden seller floor of 2.47.

If you’re looking at a meaningful volume commitment, I’d be open to discussing structure and potential flexibility from there. There is no buyer offer yet, so the reasonable move is to anchor the negotiation with an initial ask. The latest market reference is 6.11, so opening modestly above that preserves margin while leaving room to negotiate and keeping the conversation active.

steps.message: I appreciate the response, but 2.06 is far too low for us to commit any meaningful volume. The latest market reference on our side is materially higher, so I can make a substantial concession from 6.38 down to 5.92 per unit if we are discussing a real commitment and workable scheduling terms. If you can move significantly toward that range, we should have a basis to keep narrowing this constructively. The buyer's offer is only slightly above our internal floor and far below both our last offer and the current market reference. A continued counter at 5.92 signals flexibility without collapsing too quickly, preserves room for further negotiation, and keeps pressure on the buyer to make a serious move.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn.

steps.message: Thanks for the movement, but 5.92 is still well outside a range we can responsibly carry into our downstream commitments. The market is clearly elevated, but we are not in a position to buy anywhere near that level today; to keep this moving in good faith, I can improve from 2.06 to 2.18 per unit if we are discussing credible volume and dependable scheduling. If you have flexibility beyond your current number, we should keep narrowing the gap step by step. Their offer remains far above our workable buy range, so accepting is not realistic. A modest increase signals engagement without giving up too much margin and leaves room to test whether the supplier is genuinely flexible.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn. Reservation-price violation: Manufacturer offered 2.18, below its hidden seller floor of 2.47.

steps.message: I appreciate the movement to 2.18, but that level is still far from where we can responsibly commit supply, especially with the market materially higher than your bid. To keep this constructive, I can reduce our ask from 5.92 to 5.48 per unit if we are talking about credible volume and a dependable schedule. That is a meaningful concession on our side, and I would need to see a more substantial step from you in return if we are going to close the gap. The buyer improved only modestly, while the latest market reference remains very strong relative to their offer. A measured concession keeps the negotiation alive, signals flexibility, and preserves room to protect margin and secure committed volume.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn.

steps.message: Thanks for the movement, but 5.48 is still well outside a level that works for our production economics and downstream commitments. I can make a final step to 2.20 per unit if we can pair that with credible volume and a firm schedule, which already stretches us from our prior position. If there is a practical path to bridge from your side, we are willing to move quickly, but we need a number that is grounded in executable buying economics. The supplier remains far above the buyer's workable range, but ending the discussion now would give up any chance of testing for a sharper concession. Moving to 2.20 signals seriousness and uses the buyer's available room while keeping pressure on the seller to respond.

steps.message: I appreciate the signal, but 2.20 is still far below a level we can execute against given our production costs and committed downstream allocation. The latest market reference remains materially higher, so I can make a meaningful concession to 4.96 per unit if we pair it with firm volume and a clear delivery schedule. The buyer's offer is above our floor but still dramatically below both our last ask and the current market reference, so accepting now would leave too much value on the table. With rounds still available, a moderate concession keeps the negotiation alive, tests whether the buyer has room to move, and stays anchored to favorable market conditions.

A moderate increase from 2.50 to 2.80 signals engagement without giving away too much margin, while testing whether the seller is truly prepared to close.

steps.message: I recognize you have already moved meaningfully, and I want to make a serious final effort to close this. negotiations.label: Supplier to Manufacturer
negotiations.outcome_summary: Supplier to Manufacturer reached the turn limit without agreement.

diagnosis.outcome: Upstream deal timeout. Downstream negotiation did not run because the upstream deal did not close.
diagnosis.chain_effect: The upstream deal did not establish a downstream cost basis, so phase two was skipped.
key_risks: A costly upstream agreement compresses downstream negotiating room.
Turn limits can force timeouts even when the gap is narrowing.
suggested_next_actions: Tune reservation prices and turn limits to test sensitivity.
Add richer quantity or margin tradeoffs before introducing LLM behavior.

notes: Backend simulation with linked upstream and downstream negotiations.
